description: Exam 01 Final — Verbal Tenses Question Bank (1st Bimester)

questions.card_source: The professor explains the experiment results every week.
questions.question_text: The professor _____ the experiment results every week.
questions.rationale: Tests 3rd person singular -s rule in Simple Present affirmative. 'Every week' confirms habitual action.

distractor_rationale.a: Base form — correct for I/you/we/they but not for he/she/it
distractor_rationale.c: Simple Past — 'every week' signals habitual present, not past
distractor_rationale.d: Present participle — requires an auxiliary to form a valid tense

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.card_source: The system does not (doesn't) save files automatically.
questions.question_text: The system _____ save files automatically.
questions.rationale: Tests correct auxiliary for 3rd person singular negative in Simple Present (does not / doesn't).

distractor_rationale.a: don't — correct for I/you/we/they, not for 'the system' (it)
distractor_rationale.b: isn't — auxiliary for Present Continuous, wrong tense
distractor_rationale.d: hasn't — auxiliary for Present Perfect, wrong tense

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.card_source: Does the lab open at 8 AM?
questions.question_text: _____ the lab open at 8 AM?
questions.rationale: Tests correct auxiliary for 3rd person singular question in Simple Present. 'The lab' (it) requires 'Does', not 'Do'.

distractor_rationale.a: Is — auxiliary for Present Continuous questions
distractor_rationale.b: Do — correct for I/you/we/they questions, not for 'the lab' (it)
distractor_rationale.c: Has — auxiliary for Present Perfect questions

questions.instruction_pt: Escolha o auxiliar correto para completar a pergunta.

questions.card_source: The engineer finished the report yesterday.
questions.question_text: The engineer _____ the report yesterday.
questions.rationale: Tests regular verb -ed formation in Simple Past. 'Yesterday' is the canonical Simple Past time marker.

distractor_rationale.b: 3rd person Simple Present — wrong tense, contradicts 'yesterday'
distractor_rationale.d: Present Perfect — used for unspecified past experiences, incompatible with 'yesterday'

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.card_source: The students submitted their project last Friday.
questions.question_text: "The students submitted their project last Friday." — Which tense is used in this sentence?
questions.rationale: Tests recognition of the Simple Past form (-ed / past form) in context. 'Last Friday' confirms Simple Past.

distractor_rationale.a: Simple Present uses base form or 3rd-person -s (submit/submits), not 'submitted'
distractor_rationale.c: Present Perfect uses have/has + past participle (have submitted) — no specific time marker

questions.instruction_pt: Qual tempo verbal é usado nesta frase?

questions.card_source: The team is developing a new security protocol this week.
questions.question_text: The team _____ a new security protocol this week.
questions.rationale: Tests the be + verb-ing structure for Present Continuous. 'This week' signals a temporary ongoing action.

distractor_rationale.a: Base form — Simple Present, used for habits not temporary actions
distractor_rationale.b: 3rd person Simple Present — 'this week' signals ongoing activity, not habit
distractor_rationale.d: Present Perfect — implies completion, contradicts the ongoing nature of 'this week'

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.card_source: He is not (isn't) using the software right now.
questions.question_text: He _____ the software right now.
questions.rationale: Tests the is/are + not + verb-ing structure for Present Continuous negative. 'Right now' confirms the tense.

distractor_rationale.a: doesn't use — Simple Present negative; contradicts 'right now' (ongoing)
distractor_rationale.c: hasn't used — Present Perfect negative; wrong tense
distractor_rationale.d: didn't use — Simple Past negative; contradicts 'right now'

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.card_source: The technician is calibrating the equipment.
questions.question_text: "The technician is calibrating the equipment." — Which tense is used in this sentence?
questions.rationale: Tests recognition of the is + verb-ing structure. The auxiliary 'is' + '-ing' ending on the verb are the two markers.

distractor_rationale.a: Simple Present: 'The technician calibrates the equipment' (no auxiliary + -ing)
distractor_rationale.c: Simple Past: 'The technician calibrated the equipment'
distractor_rationale.d: Present Perfect: 'The technician has calibrated the equipment'

questions.instruction_pt: Qual tempo verbal é usado nesta frase?

questions.card_source: The committee will announce the results next Monday.
questions.question_text: The committee _____ the results next Monday.
questions.rationale: Tests the 'will + base verb' structure for Simple Future. 'Next Monday' is the key future time marker.

distractor_rationale.a: announces — Simple Present 3rd person; 'next Monday' requires future, not habitual
distractor_rationale.b: is announcing — Present Continuous; can express near-future plans but less standard with 'next Monday'
distractor_rationale.c: has announced — Present Perfect; implies completed action, contradicts 'next Monday'

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.question_text: Which time expression matches the Present Continuous tense?
questions.rationale: Tests knowledge of time adverbs as tense markers. 'Right now' is the canonical Present Continuous marker, contrasting with markers for Simple Past (last night), Simple Present (every Monday), and Simple Future (next year).

distractor_rationale.a: last night — signals Simple Past (completed past event)
distractor_rationale.b: every Monday — signals Simple Present (habitual action)
distractor_rationale.d: next year — signals Simple Future (will / going to)

questions.instruction_pt: Qual expressão de tempo corresponde ao Present Continuous?

questions.card_source: The researchers were collecting data when the power went out.
questions.question_text: The researchers _____ data when the power went out.
questions.rationale: Tests the were + verb-ing structure for Past Continuous. The sentence presents an ongoing past action ('collecting') interrupted by a shorter event ('went out').

distractor_rationale.a: collected — Simple Past; treats both actions as sequential, not overlapping
distractor_rationale.b: have collected — Present Perfect; wrong time frame
distractor_rationale.d: had collected — Past Perfect; implies completion before another past event, not an ongoing action

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.card_source: She was studying when I called.
questions.question_text: Which Past Continuous sentence is correct?

options.text: She was study when I called.
She was studying when I called.
She were studying when I called.
She was studied when I called.

questions.rationale: Tests the Past Continuous formation (was/were + verb-ing). Each distractor targets a specific structural error.

distractor_rationale.a: was + base form — missing the -ing ending required in Past Continuous
distractor_rationale.c: were — incorrect auxiliary for singular subject 'she' (requires 'was')
distractor_rationale.d: was + past participle — confuses Present Perfect (has studied) with Past Continuous

questions.instruction_pt: Qual frase está correta?

questions.question_text:
Choose the correct option:

"While the professor _____ the lesson, a student _____ the room."
questions.rationale: Tests the classic Past Continuous / Simple Past contrast: the longer background action uses Past Continuous (was explaining); the shorter interrupting action uses Simple Past (entered).

distractor_rationale.b: Reverses the roles — 'explained' treats the lesson as a brief event; 'was entering' makes the student's action the ongoing background
distractor_rationale.c: Both Past Continuous — valid only if both actions were simultaneous and ongoing, which is not the intended reading
distractor_rationale.d: Both Simple Past — treats both actions as sequential, losing the 'while' relationship

questions.instruction_pt: Escolha a combinação correta de tempos verbais.

questions.card_source: She has learned three programming languages so far.
questions.question_text: She _____ three programming languages so far.
questions.rationale: Tests the have/has + past participle structure for Present Perfect. 'So far' signals an accumulating experience up to now — classic Present Perfect context.

distractor_rationale.a: learns — Simple Present; 'so far' signals accumulation over time, not a habit
distractor_rationale.b: learned — Simple Past; would require a specific time marker (e.g., 'last year'), not 'so far'
distractor_rationale.c: was learning — Past Continuous; implies interrupted ongoing action in the past, not accumulated experience

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.question_text:
Choose the correct tense for each blank:

"I _____ this book (a) LAST YEAR. I _____ it (b) THREE TIMES."
questions.rationale: Tests the core PP vs Simple Past distinction: 'last year' (specific time) requires Simple Past; 'three times' (unspecified accumulated experience) requires Present Perfect.

distractor_rationale.b: Reverses the rule — 'have read last year' is ungrammatical; Present Perfect rejects specific past time markers
distractor_rationale.c: All Simple Past — 'read three times' is possible but weak; misses the contrast intended by 'three times'
distractor_rationale.d: All Present Perfect — 'have read last year' is ungrammatical

questions.instruction_pt: Escolha a combinação correta de tempos verbais para as duas frases.

questions.question_text: Which sentence uses the Present Perfect correctly?

options.text: I have finished the report yesterday.
I have finished the report last week.

questions.rationale: Tests the rule that Present Perfect cannot be used with specific past time markers. 'Yesterday' and 'last week' require Simple Past. 'Already' is a valid Present Perfect marker — making option D grammatically correct, but the question tests error detection in A and C.

distractor_rationale.a: 'have finished yesterday' — Present Perfect + specific time marker 'yesterday' is ungrammatical
distractor_rationale.c: 'have finished last week' — same error with 'last week'
distractor_rationale.d: 'finished already' — grammatically possible (Simple Past + already is acceptable in American English) but less standard than Present Perfect; included to test precision. Option B is the cleanest, unambiguous correct answer

questions.instruction_pt: Qual frase está correta?

questions.card_source: She is going to study cybersecurity next semester.
questions.question_text: She _____ study cybersecurity next semester.
questions.rationale: Tests the am/is/are + going to + base verb structure for future plans. This represents a pre-arranged plan (she has already decided), the classic 'going to' context.

distractor_rationale.a: 'will going to' — double future auxiliary; ungrammatical
distractor_rationale.c: 'goes to' — Simple Present 3rd person; 'next semester' signals future, not habitual present
distractor_rationale.d: 'going to' alone — missing the 'is' auxiliary; grammatically incomplete

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.card_source: They are not going to use that method anymore.
questions.question_text: They _____ use that method anymore.
questions.rationale: Tests the are/is/am + not + going to structure for negating a future plan. The plural subject 'they' requires 'aren't going to'.

distractor_rationale.a: 'won't going to' — mixes 'will' negative with 'going to'; ungrammatical
distractor_rationale.c: 'don't going to' — Simple Present auxiliary with 'going to'; ungrammatical
distractor_rationale.d: 'haven't going to' — Present Perfect auxiliary with 'going to'; ungrammatical

questions.instruction_pt: Complete a frase com a forma correta do verbo.

questions.question_text:
Ana looks at the dark clouds outside and says: "_____ rain."

Which option correctly completes her sentence?
questions.rationale: Tests the will vs going to distinction. 'Going to' is used for predictions based on visible present evidence (dark clouds). 'Will' is used for spontaneous decisions or predictions without current evidence.

distractor_rationale.a: 'will rain' — valid for a general prediction or spontaneous statement but ignores the evidence (dark clouds) that specifically calls for 'going to'
distractor_rationale.c: 'it rains' — Simple Present; used for facts/habits (e.g., 'it rains a lot in winter'), not for in-the-moment predictions
distractor_rationale.d: 'it is raining' — Present Continuous; describes what is happening now, but the clouds signal future rain, not current rain

questions.instruction_pt: Escolha a forma correta para expressar uma previsão baseada em evidência visual.

questions.question_text:
Read the passage:

"Every Friday, the lab team meets to review progress. Last Friday, they tested a new sensor. They are currently writing the final report. They have already submitted the project budget."

Which sentence uses the Simple Past?

options.text: The lab team meets to review progress.
They are currently writing the final report.
They have already submitted the project budget.

questions.rationale: Tests tense recognition across four tenses in a single passage: Simple Present (every Friday), Simple Past (last Friday), Present Continuous (currently), and Present Perfect (already). The time marker 'Last Friday' confirms Simple Past.

distractor_rationale.a: Simple Present — 'every Friday' is the canonical habitual-action marker
distractor_rationale.c: Present Continuous — 'currently' + is/are + -ing confirms ongoing action
distractor_rationale.d: Present Perfect — 'already' + have/has + past participle confirms recent completed action

questions.instruction_pt: Leia o texto e identifique qual frase usa o Simple Past.
